jokes.text: You know what? He owes me $50 from golf. That's $50 you're never gonna see. Never! Never gonna see that 50. Never ever ever ever. You can't ask a mourner for $50.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Funkhouser's mother's tragic death
jokes.punchline: Larry's immediate pivot to money owed, escalating repetition of 'never'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry David character moment - inappropriate focus on money during tragedy. The escalating 'never' repetition builds comedy effectively.

jokes.text: I am so much closer with Marty than you are. I've known him so much longer. However long you've known him is irrelevant in this equation.
jokes.setup: Both waiting for the other to offer condolences first
jokes.punchline: Competing over who deserves condolences more for someone else's loss
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry pettiness over social protocols. Good character work but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Well, that's great, Larry, but I wouldn't take so much credit, because it's not your DNA.
jokes.setup: Larry bragging about teaching kids multiplication
jokes.punchline: Susie cuts him down by pointing out they're adopted
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback from Susie deflating Larry's pride. Good economy and character work.

jokes.text: Really? Oh, yeah, I'll be nice the whole day. And then we'll have sex. And then I'll just go back to being the way I am.
jokes.setup: Larry explains why he's being nice on some days
jokes.punchline: Completely transactional view of niceness for sex
jokes.explanation: Brutally honest Larry insight into marriage dynamics. Great economy and quotable delivery.

jokes.text: I love these people that come here, they get 10 samples, you know? And it's not right for the woman working back there. She's got better things to do than just scooping out samples for them.
jokes.setup: Larry watching ice cream shop operations
jokes.punchline: His self-righteous concern for the worker while criticizing customers
jokes.explanation: Sets up the sampling conflict. Typical Larry social observation but not particularly original.

jokes.text: You know, you're like a sample abuser. That's what you are. You're abusing your sampling privileges.
jokes.punchline: Larry's confrontation escalates to 'sample abuser' terminology
jokes.explanation: Great Larry neologism - 'sample abuser' is perfectly absurd yet logical. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Vanilla! She winds up with vanilla. You got to be kidding me.
jokes.punchline: She chooses the most basic flavor after all that
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the sampling sequence. The anticlimax is beautifully executed with great economy.

jokes.text: I'm an orphan. You're a what? I'm an orphan. Orphan? Yeah, an orphan! You're a little too old to be an orphan.
jokes.punchline: Larry's literal interpretation of 'orphan' age limits
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic debate. Larry's literal-minded approach to language creates genuine surprise and quotability.

jokes.text: You could be 70 and be an orphan? You could be 100 and be an orphan! You can't be 100 and be an orphan. Yeah, you can! Okay. Little Orphan Funkhouser.
jokes.punchline: Escalation to 100-year-old orphan, ending with 'Little Orphan Funkhouser'
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation with cultural reference callback. 'Little Orphan Funkhouser' is comedy gold.

jokes.text: Look, there's a little drip. Get it! Oh, another drip! Got it!
jokes.punchline: Manic vigilance about drips while talking to mourning friend
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy moment showing Larry's obsessive nature, but not particularly original or memorable.

jokes.text: Taking off my shoe. What?! I got money in the shoe.
jokes.setup: Funkhouser reaching for money to pay debt
jokes.punchline: Reveals he keeps money in his running shoe
jokes.explanation: Good visual absurdity - who keeps money in their shoe while jogging? Sets up the sweaty money running gag.

jokes.text: Take the $50! No, I don't want it. I'd really rather not. I don't want that 50 from your sweaty disgusting sock and sneaker.
jokes.punchline: Larry's visceral disgust creates standoff over payment method
jokes.explanation: Great cringe comedy - the absurdity of refusing payment due to hygiene concerns. Sets up major plot thread.

jokes.text: Fold it-- that's it, okay. Put it in my pocket. Yeah, okay. Put it in there.
jokes.punchline: Awkward choreography of avoiding contact
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy of contamination avoidance, mildly amusing

jokes.text: You just brought some ice cream just for Cheryl? You didn't bring none to share with us?
jokes.punchline: Loretta's pointed questions about Larry's selfishness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Loretta calling out Larry's thoughtlessness. Not groundbreaking but effective.

jokes.text: See the difference? Sin of commission, omission. That's a sin of omission, much better than the commission.
jokes.punchline: Pseudo-intellectual categorization of his selfishness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry rationalization - using philosophical concepts to justify basic selfishness. Great character integration.

jokes.text: This woman that he's talking about is the Dean of Admissions at Dryden School. That nice school we was trying to get the kids in? Yeah. Now, there's a problem with their application.
jokes.setup: Larry's ice cream store confrontation story
jokes.punchline: Reveal that the woman was the school dean
jokes.explanation: Excellent plot development reveal. The irony is perfectly structured - Larry's petty concern creates major consequences.

jokes.text: Like at night, you tiptoe. That's an unwritten rule. You tiptoe so you don't wake people up. You tiptoe. There's no sign, 'Tiptoe.'
jokes.punchline: Increasingly passionate defense of tiptoeing as social contract
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry social philosophy. Good escalation and character work, though the concept isn't groundbreaking.

jokes.text: I'm a great rectifier. That's what I do. I break down, I rectify.
jokes.setup: Larry promising to fix the school situation
jokes.punchline: Self-aggrandizing description of his problem-solving pattern
jokes.explanation: Great Larry self-assessment. 'Rectifier' is a perfect word choice - technical yet pompous. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I've been apologizing to people since I'm six years old on a daily basis. I pretty much have it down.
jokes.punchline: Larry's defensive claim of apology expertise
jokes.explanation: Excellent character insight - Larry's entire life as one long series of apologies. Self-aware yet delusional.

jokes.text: So I'm kinda be charged for what, like a half hour of your toilet time?
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate focus on bathroom time in professional interaction
jokes.explanation: Typical Larry social awkwardness - making bathroom habits a business negotiation. Good cringe factor.

jokes.text: No, I just don't know where you dragged that thing out of. I mean, you know, that's...
jokes.punchline: Painter's identical reaction to Larry's earlier disgust
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure - the cursed money continues its journey. Shows Larry's hypocrisy.

jokes.text: Hey, Funkhouser. Call me back. No one's taking this 50. I can't get rid of it. I'll be home in an hour.
jokes.punchline: Larry's voicemail treating the $50 like a curse
jokes.explanation: Good running gag development. The money becomes a comedic albatross around Larry's neck.

jokes.text: As you know, my wife and I took in a homeless family who were victims of Hurricane Edna.
jokes.punchline: Opening with completely fabricated sob story
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry manipulation - opening with complete fiction delivered earnestly. Great character work.

jokes.text: they call me daddy. Oh, really? Yeah, it's really sweet. That's nice. That's very nice. I really love being called daddy. I don't particularly like children, that's why I don't really have any. But I like being called daddy.
jokes.setup: Larry's fabricated story about homeless family
jokes.punchline: Admitting he doesn't like children while claiming to love being called daddy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Larry psychology - wanting the title without the responsibility. The contradiction is perfectly Larry and highly quotable.

jokes.text: It's just really refreshing. It's really rare. Saying all that, I am a pretty good person. I did take in a homeless family.
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately contradicting the humble assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry moment - unable to accept praise without immediately undermining it with self-aggrandizement.

jokes.text: Fabric samples. They're so beautiful. What are you doing with the fabrics? Oh, I'm gonna redo this couch. Is this the one I chose? Uh, no, actually. Wasn't that the one that you wanted? Oh, yes, but now I want this.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to leave after successful apology
jokes.punchline: Getting trapped in indecisive fabric selection conversation
jokes.explanation: Standard Larry trapped-in-conversation scenario. Not particularly innovative but serves plot function.

jokes.text: Come on upstairs. Go upstairs? Come on. Let me put it in some water. Yeah, that can wait just one second.
jokes.punchline: Transparent attempt to cash in on good deed
jokes.explanation: Callback to Larry's transactional approach to marriage

jokes.text: Someone stole flowers that were marking the spot where my mother was hit in her wheelchair.
jokes.punchline: Setting up the reveal that Larry is the thief
jokes.explanation: Good setup for major reveal. The dramatic irony works well - audience knows Larry's guilt.

jokes.text: I keep track of it. I drive by there 10 times a day.
jokes.setup: Larry asks how he'd know flowers are missing
jokes.punchline: Funkhouser's obsessive memorial monitoring
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing grief obsession

jokes.text: There they are. I knew it! Where'd you get the flowers? Where'd you get those?
jokes.punchline: His detective work leading to immediate confrontation
jokes.explanation: Excellent dramatic escalation. The smell-based detection is both logical and comedically satisfying.

jokes.text: They wouldn't take the 50 at the flower store! How could you do that?! Why? There are so many of them. So many of them?! They're not there to pick!
jokes.setup: Larry's weak defense of flower theft
jokes.punchline: Connecting contaminated money to flower theft
jokes.explanation: Brilliant convergence of multiple plot threads

jokes.text: How many flowers does she need? Are these from a graveyard? Not a graveyard. It's a roadside memorial. It's not such a-- come on!
jokes.setup: Larry being confronted about the theft
jokes.punchline: His weak justification and distinction between memorial types
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry rationalization - making legalistic distinctions to minimize his offense. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: I am missing one! Where's the third bunch? There were three bouquets.
jokes.punchline: Realization about missing third bouquet
jokes.explanation: Good escalation setting up dean office revelation

jokes.text: If you weren't my best friend, I would take my bare hands and pop your head off your neck. He's not my best friend.
jokes.punchline: Larry's petty correction about friendship status
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Larry moment - facing death threats, he focuses on correcting relationship terminology. Perfect character priority inversion.

jokes.text: I do like that perfume Belle Fille. Belle Fille. That would make me happy.
jokes.setup: Larry asking what he can do to make amends
jokes.punchline: Cheryl's specific, transactional response about expensive perfume
jokes.explanation: Sets up the perfume store sequence. Functional relationship dynamics but not particularly innovative.

jokes.text: You know? You can't sample everything in the world. It's just impossible. We'd all like to try everything. Even though I'd love to, just can't. There's people in line, people got to go places.
jokes.punchline: Larry's return to sampling philosophy in different context
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Larry's consistent worldview. The escalation to universal sampling philosophy is effective.

jokes.text: I'd like to be with a lot of women, I can't sample every woman. You know what I'm talking about?
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate sexual metaphor in perfume store
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry escalation - taking reasonable principle to inappropriate extreme. Good shock value while staying in character.

jokes.text: Sorry, I think that's the last one. I was on line ahead of him. I was ahead of him. Yeah, well, you shouldn't have changed lines.
jokes.punchline: Larry's justification for line-cutting victory
jokes.explanation: Good Larry justification for questionable behavior

jokes.text: You know, there's a way to fix that too. Like they do in Disneyland, you know, how the line snakes around. Go into a deli, you take a number.
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about unfair perfume store lines
jokes.punchline: Elaborate solutions for minor retail inconveniences
jokes.explanation: Typical Larry over-engineering of social systems. Good character consistency but not exceptional comedy.

jokes.text: This is fucking great, Larry! Now Sammy's kicked out too. Just knowing you is a liability.
jokes.setup: Discovery that Larry retrieved the flowers from dean's office
jokes.punchline: Jeff's realization that Larry's toxicity is spreading
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - Larry's chaos spreads beyond his own life. 'Just knowing you is a liability' is perfectly put and quotable.

jokes.text: You want the flowers? Take the fucking flowers!
jokes.punchline: Jeff angrily hurling the mangled bouquet
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy moment. Jeff's frustrated violence against innocent flowers is satisfying release.

jokes.text: They should all stand up. That's what flowers do when they're healthy. Stop picking up the petals, okay?
jokes.punchline: Funkhouser's grief-fueled flower quality assessment
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Funkhouser's obsessive care about memorial flower condition shows his grief.

jokes.text: God, look at these vultures going for that food! These-- these people, they don't even chew. You know, I bet if you threw a quarter down, there'd be a stampede.
jokes.punchline: Comparing mourners to scavenging animals
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry inappropriate observation at solemn event. Dark but funny social commentary.

jokes.text: Do you smell something? Yes, what is that? That's Mom's perfume. You're right. It's so strong. It's like she's here with us.
jokes.punchline: Detecting the stolen perfume Larry bought Cheryl
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback structure - Larry's guilt literally haunts the funeral. The irony of the mother's 'presence' being Larry's mistake is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: Hey, somebody dropped a 50!
jokes.setup: The cursed $50 bill throughout the episode
jokes.punchline: Finally being discovered at the funeral
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending callback. The money that couldn't be given away finally finds a home at the funeral - poetic justice.
